coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and zinc doped carbon quantum dots for rapid and accurate detection of mercury ions in cosmetics

Jin, Hao
Lu, Yue
Hu, Juan
Yue, Xiaona
Chen, Hong
Zhu, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and reliable detection technologies for mercury ion in cosmetics are critical for public health. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen and zinc doped carbon quantum dots (N, Zn-CDs) with bright and stable fluorescence using the hydrothermal method. Structure characterization reveals the abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N, Zn-CDs, which readily coordinate with Hg2+, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Basing on this, a rapid and simple quantitative analysis method for Hg2+ detection was designed. By optimizing the detection conditions, a good linear relationship in the range of 0 to 10 μM and a low detection limit of 0.023 μM were achieved. Besides, interference tests demonstrate the presence of other metal ions does not interfere with Hg2+ detection. When detecting Hg2+ in four types of cosmetics spiked with the ion, the sensor achieved recovery rates of 98.27 %, 110.03 %, 100.90 %, and 108.80 %, respectively, with relative standard deviations all under 6.90 %, demonstrating its capability to identify Hg2+ in complex matrices. This research offers a novel approach for the rapid and accurate detection of mercury in cosmetics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image (a), X-ray diffraction (b) and high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p (c) and N 1 s (d) of N, Zn-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N, Zn-CDs solutions with different metal ions, (b) comparison of fluorescence quenching rates of different metal ions, (c) fluorescence spectra in the presence of Hg2+ of 0–50 μM and (d) linear relationship between fluorescence quenching degree and Hg2+concentration.

(a) Schematic of the detecting process of the cosmetics, (b) recovery rates of four different cosmetics, (c) fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution of N, Zn-CDs and Sulwhasoo with Hg2+ of 0–5 μM.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and zinc doped carbon quantum dots for rapid and accurate detection of mercury ions in cosmetics

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and reliable detection technologies for mercury ion in cosmetics are critical for public health. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen and zinc doped carbon quantum dots (N, Zn-CDs) with bright and stable fluorescence using the hydrothermal method. Structure characterization reveals the abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N, Zn-CDs, which readily coordinate with Hg2+, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Basing on this, a rapid and simple quantitative analysis method for Hg2+ detection was designed. By optimizing the detection conditions, a good linear relationship in the range of 0 to 10 μM and a low detection limit of 0.023 μM were achieved. Besides, interference tests demonstrate the presence of other metal ions does not interfere with Hg2+ detection. When detecting Hg2+ in four types of cosmetics spiked with the ion, the sensor achieved recovery rates of 98.27 %, 110.03 %, 100.90 %, and 108.80 %, respectively, with relative standard deviations all under 6.90 %, demonstrating its capability to identify Hg2+ in complex matrices. This research offers a novel approach for the rapid and accurate detection of mercury in cosmetics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion contamination in cosmetics, especially in skin-lightening products, occurs frequently due to its ability to inhibit melanin production. It has been a critical issue for public health as mercury can cause neurological and kidney damage, dermatological effects and reproductive and developmental harm [1]. Therefore, the addition is strictly prohibited by European Union and is limited to 1 ppm in China and America. Rapid and reliable detection is necessary for safeguarding public health.

Current methodologies for detecting mercury ions, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [2], atomic absorption spectroscopy [3], electrochemical sensor [4–7], and liquid chromatography [8], offer high sensitivity and specificity but come with drawbacks, such as the need for expensive equipment, complex sample preparation, and extended analysis times.

Recently, nanomaterials have gained widespread attention in sensing applications due to their high surface area, unique optical, electrical, and magnetic properties, and specific surface functionalization that enables the selective detection of analytes [9,10]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have exceptional photoluminescent properties, making them ideal for use in fluorescence-based sensing applications [11]. Their small size, ease of modification, biocompatibility, and strong luminescent properties facilitate the development of rapid, sensitive, and selective sensors for mercury detection [12]. Despite their advantages, the performance of CDs in complex matrices like cosmetics is often hindered by the presence of various interfering substances [13]. Doping with other elements introduces additional functional groups or electronic properties, potentially increasing the interaction between CDs and target analytes, thus improving selectivity and sensitivity [14].

In this study, we synthesized nitrogen and zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (N, Zn-CDs) with bright and stable fluorescence via a green heat treatment method. The abundance of active functional groups on the surface of N, Zn-CDs, such as hydroxy and amino groups, facilitates the chelation with Hg2+, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Basing on this principle, we designed a simple sensitive and selective detection method for Hg2+, providing an effective approach for Hg2+ detection in cosmetic products.

The synthesis conditions have been optimized as shown in Fig. S1. Citric acid and zinc nitrate were mixed in a mass ratio of 1:0.4 and dissolved in 25 mL of water through ultrasonication for 5 min. Subsequently, 2.6 mL of ethylenediamine was added and stirred in a reaction vessel at a constant temperature ranging from 185 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was then subjected to centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 8 min to remove insoluble particles, followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate small impurities. The resulting N, Zn-CDs were stored in a refrigerator as a reserve liquid for subsequent experiments.

27 μL of N, Zn-CDs solution was added to a sodium citrate-citric acid buffer solution (0.01 M, pH = 6.5). Metal ion solutions were added to a final volume of 1.6 mL and then mixed and incubated for 2 min followed by fluorescence detection at optimized conditions (Fig. S2). Essence waters from four brands, including Ximuyuan, Lingboshi, Guyu, and Sulwhasoo (see Fig. S3 for their packaging and Table S1 for their ingredients), were spiked with mercury ions at a concentration of 4 μM to simulate cosmetics containing mercury ions and then tested.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S4) shows uniform spherical morphology of the N, Zn-CDs with minimal aggregation. The fringe spacings of 0.33 nm shown in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image are corresponding to 005 plane of carbon lattice (Fig. 1 a). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 1b) reveals a carbon lattice (002) peak at 26.38° and a weak ZnO (110) peak at 56.60°.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis reveals the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and zinc (Fig. S5), which is further confirmed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra in Fig. S6a, demonstrating the successful synthesis of N, Zn-CDs. The deconvoluted Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 1c) exhibits two peaks at 1020.36 eV and 1043.43 eV, corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 energy levels, respectively. This indicates that the zinc species are in a divalent state and confirming the successful incorporation of zinc ions. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. S6b), O 1s (Fig. S6c) and N 1s (Fig. 1d) reveal abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surfaces of N, Zn-CDs, which are prone to coordinate with the Hg2+. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. S7) shows a broad absorption peak in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H. These functional groups enhance the hydrophilicity and stability of the N, Zn-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of N, Zn-CDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To study the fluorescence quenching behavior of the N, Zn-CDs solution towards various metal ions, metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ag+, Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ were added to the N, Zn-CDs solution under optimized conditions (more details are provided in the Supplemental Material), and changes in fluorescence intensity were measured (Fig. 2 a and b). Notably, the addition of Hg2+ led to significant fluorescence quenching, while other metal cations caused almost no change in fluorescence intensity. This can be attributed to the abundance of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N, Zn-CDs, which enable strong chelation with Hg2+ ions and selective fluorescence quenching. Moreover, when Hg2+ was spiked together with other metal cations, the fluorescence spectrum remained unchanged compared to when only Hg2+ was spiked (Fig. S8). This indicates that the presence of other metal ions does not interfere with the detection of Hg2+.

Additionally, a linear relationship was observed between the Hg2+ concentration (0–10 μM) and the fluorescence quenching rate of N, Zn-CDs (Fig. 2c and 2d). The calibration equation obtained was y = 0.0460 + 0.0174x, with an R2 of 0.9947, and the KSV was calculated to be 9.9987 × 104 mol−1⋅L. The method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.023 μM, demonstrating superior sensitivity and linearity compared to other methods in the literature (Table S2).

To assess the applicability of the N, Zn-CDs sensor in detecting Hg2+ in complex matrix, four cosmetic products, Ximuyuan, Lingboshi, Guyu, and Sulwhasoo, with Hg2+ standardly spiked to 4 μM were detected (Fig. 3 a). The recovery rates of Ximuyuan, Lingboshi, Guyu, and Sulwhasoo are 98.27 %, 110.03 %, 100.9 %, and 108.8 %, respectively (Fig. 3b). The relative standard deviations (RSD) for all samples are below 6.90 % (see Table S3 for detailed results). Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution of Sulwhasoo and N, Zn-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ of 0–5 μM (Fig. 3c) show linear decrease of fluorescence intensity with the Hg2+ concentration. These results demonstrate the sensor has excellent resistance to matrix interference when detecting Hg2+ in real samples.

In conclusion, highly fluorescent N, Zn-CDs with abundant hydroxyl and amino groups were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. These active functional groups enable strong and selective chelation with Hg2+ ions, resulting in fluorescence quenching. This enables the design of a rapid and simple sensor for Hg2+ detection with a good linear relationship in the range of 0 to 10 μM and a low LOD of 0.023 μM. When applied to detect Hg2+ in complex cosmetics, the sensor demonstrates good recoveries, proving its excellent accuracy in detecting samples with complex matrices.

Ye Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hao Jin: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yue Lu: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Juan Hu: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaona Yue: Validation, Software. Hong Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Jing Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Key Research and Development Plan of Anhui Province (No. 2022e07020037), Health Research Project of Anhui Province (No. AHWJ2022b014), Shen-Nong Scholar Program of Anhui Agricultural University (No. rc382101), Importing and Stabilizing Talent Research Funding Project of Anhui Agricultural University (No. rc382004).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136809.
